entries.description: Engaging the large back muscles (rhomboids, latissimus dorsi) rather than biceps ensures a stronger, more consistent draw and reduces shoulder fatigue. This minimizes draw shoulder rise.
root_causes: Lack of awareness of back muscles
observable_signs: Draw shoulder rises significantly during the draw
Draw elbow collapses inward prematurely
Bow arm drops during draw
coaching_cues: Keep your shoulder down and back

drill.name: Resistance Band Draw
drill.description: Use a light resistance band to practice the drawing motion, focusing on initiating the pull with the back muscles and keeping the draw shoulder low. Hold at full draw for 3-5 seconds.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw length, poor anchor, and premature fatigue, causing vertical stringing and wide groups.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder spine draw_shoulder_rise 15-50mm error drawing with arm muscles pull with your back squeeze shoulder blades together inconsistent draw length poor anchor fatigue vertical stringing

entries.description: Maintaining a high draw elbow, roughly in line with the arrow, facilitates proper back tension and ensures a consistent anchor point. A low elbow indicates poor back engagement (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: Draw elbow angle is too acute (collapsed)
Draw shoulder is higher than the bow shoulder
coaching_cues: Point your elbow at the target
Line up your elbow with the arrow

drill.name: Mirror Draw Check
drill.description: Stand in front of a mirror and practice your draw, paying close attention to the height and angle of your draw elbow. Ensure it stays high and aligned with the arrow path.

entries.score_impact: Hinders proper back tension, leads to an inconsistent anchor, and can cause string clearance issues, affecting arrow grouping.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_elbow draw_shoulder draw_elbow_angle 60-85 degrees error low draw elbow elbow up and back line up elbow with arrow inconsistent anchor string clearance

entries.description: Locking or hyperextending the bow elbow can lead to string slap, injury, and inconsistent bow arm stability. A slight bend (170-178 degrees) is ideal for shock absorption and safety (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Lack of awareness of elbow position
observable_signs: Bow arm elbow angle is greater than 180 degrees
Elbow joint appears 'locked'
coaching_cues: Slight bend in your bow arm

drill.name: Elbow Bend Check
drill.description: Practice drawing and holding, consciously maintaining a slight bend in your bow arm. Have a partner or camera check your elbow angle from the side to ensure it's not locked.

entries.score_impact: Increases risk of string slap on the forearm, causes inconsistent bow arm stability, and can lead to joint injury, impacting accuracy.
entries.embed_text: draw bow_elbow bow_arm_extension_angle 181-190 degrees error hyperextended bow elbow soft elbow slight bend in bow arm string slap injury inconsistent bow arm stability

entries.description: Leaning back during the draw compromises balance and makes it difficult to engage the back muscles effectively. A neutral, vertical spine posture is crucial for stability and consistent force application (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Attempting to compensate for heavy draw weight
observable_signs: Spine forward lean angle is significantly negative (leaning away from target)
Hip position shifts backward

drill.name: Wall Stance Drill
drill.description: Stand with your back against a wall, ensuring your heels, hips, and shoulders touch the wall. Practice your draw motion while maintaining contact with the wall to reinforce a vertical spine.

entries.score_impact: Leads to balance issues, inconsistent anchor point, and difficulty achieving proper back tension, resulting in erratic arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw spine hip spine_forward_lean -15 to -5 degrees error spine leaning back stand tall maintain vertical posture balance issues inconsistent anchor erratic arrow placement

entries.description: Tilting the head sideways at anchor creates an inconsistent reference point for the string and sight picture, making consistent aiming difficult. The head should remain upright and stable (estimated ranges).
root_causes: Trying to 'find' the anchor point with the head
observable_signs: Head tilt angle is significantly non-zero (tilted left or right)

drill.name: Head Position Check
drill.description: Practice drawing and anchoring in front of a mirror, ensuring your head remains perfectly upright and level. Focus on feeling the string contact points without moving your head.

entries.score_impact: Directly causes an inconsistent anchor point and sight picture, leading to horizontal stringing and wide groups on the target.
entries.embed_text: anchor head spine head_tilt 5-15 degrees error head tilting sideways head up and still look straight ahead inconsistent anchor sight picture horizontal stringing

entries.description: A bent or 'broken' draw wrist at anchor compromises the consistency of the string release and can alter the draw length. A flat wrist ensures a clean release and consistent anchor pressure.
root_causes: Incorrect hand position on the string
observable_signs: Draw wrist angle is significantly flexed or extended
Knuckles are not in line with the forearm
coaching_cues: Straight line from knuckles to elbow

drill.name: Flat Wrist Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing and anchoring, focusing on keeping the draw wrist perfectly flat. Use a small ruler or straight edge placed on the back of your hand and forearm to check for straightness.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent string release, affecting arrow flight and causing vertical or horizontal stringing on the target.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_wrist draw_hand error broken draw wrist flat wrist straight line from knuckles to elbow inconsistent string release vertical horizontal stringing

entries.description: A smooth, continuous draw motion prevents muscle fatigue and ensures consistent shot timing. Stopping or stuttering mid-draw disrupts rhythm and increases effort.
root_causes: Insufficient draw weight for archer's strength
observable_signs: Velocity of draw_hand or draw_elbow drops to zero or near zero mid-draw
Jerky movements in joint angles during draw

drill.name: Slow Motion Draw
drill.description: Practice drawing the bow very slowly and deliberately, focusing on maintaining continuous movement without any pauses or jerks. Feel the engagement of your back muscles throughout.

entries.score_impact: Causes inconsistent shot timing, increased muscle fatigue, and difficulty achieving a consistent anchor, leading to varied arrow placement.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_elbow bow_shoulder bow_elbow error stuttering draw motion smooth and continuous one fluid motion inconsistent shot timing fatigue varied arrow placement

entries.description: Raising the bow arm to the correct height before initiating the draw ensures proper alignment and reduces unnecessary movement during the draw phase. Starting low requires extra effort and movement.
root_causes: Lack of awareness of pre-draw position
Attempting to 'push' the bow up during the draw
observable_signs: Bow arm starts significantly below target height
Bow shoulder rises considerably during the initial draw phase
coaching_cues: Get to full height before pulling

drill.name: Pre-Draw Hold
drill.description: Nock an arrow and raise your bow arm to the correct height and extension, holding it steady for 2-3 seconds before starting your draw. Focus on stability.

entries.score_impact: Leads to inconsistent draw mechanics, potential for poor alignment, and increased effort, which can affect shot consistency over time.
entries.embed_text: setup bow_shoulder bow_elbow draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_height_diff 30-60mm error bow arm starts low set your bow arm first bow arm up then draw inconsistent draw mechanics poor alignment

entries.description: Minimizing the upward movement of the draw shoulder during the draw is critical for engaging the back muscles and maintaining a stable anchor. Excessive rise indicates arm-dominant drawing (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: Draw shoulder height increases significantly from setup to anchor
Draw shoulder appears 'shrugged'

drill.name: Shoulder Depression Drill
drill.description: Practice drawing while consciously trying to push your draw shoulder down and back. Use a mirror to observe and ensure minimal upward movement of the shoulder.

entries.score_impact: Compromises back tension, leads to an inconsistent anchor height, increases fatigue, and can cause injury, severely impacting accuracy and consistency.
entries.embed_text: draw draw_shoulder draw_shoulder_rise 20-50mm error excessive draw shoulder rise keep your shoulder down depress your shoulder blade poor back tension inconsistent anchor fatigue injury

entries.description: A consistent anchor point, where the string hand repeatedly touches the same spot on the face/jaw, is fundamental for repeatable aiming and arrow placement. Variability leads to wide groups (estimated ranges).
observable_signs: High standard deviation in draw_hand 3D position at full draw across multiple shots
Variations in head_tilt or draw_wrist angle at anchor
coaching_cues: Don't move your head to find the anchor

drill.name: Blind Bale Anchor
drill.description: Shoot at a blank bale from a close distance (3-5m) with eyes closed or blurred vision, focusing solely on feeling the anchor point consistently. Open eyes only after release.

entries.score_impact: The most critical factor for accuracy; inconsistency in the anchor point directly causes wide groups, especially vertical and horizontal stringing.
entries.embed_text: anchor draw_hand head anchor_point_consistency 10-30mm error inconsistent anchor point solid anchor every time feel same contact points wide groups vertical horizontal stringing
